0: <title>Applying soil quality indicators in the context of life cycle assessment in a Finnish case study </title>
Received: 30 October 2015 / Accepted: 29 November 2016 / Published online: 10 December 2016 Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 2016
1: Purpose In this paper, we present a case study of soil quality assessments for Finnish arable crop production. The aim was to assess the applicability of three soil quality indicators by testing available assessment models and data in the context of a life cycle assessment (LCA). The indicators were erosion, soil organic matter (SOM) and compaction, which are crucial effects of soil cultivation in Finland, strongly affecting the cultivation fitness of soil especially in the long run.

Methods In this case study, we assessed the effects of four Finnish arable crop rotations. The functional unit was 1 metric ton of cereal or turnip rape seed on the farm. The model used for assessing SOM was Yasso07, VIHMA for erosion and COMPSOIL for compaction. We used data from two conven- tional and two organic farms, collected from 4-year-long crop rotations that included turnip rape, cereals and green manure ley. Farm data were supplemented with geographic informa- tion and data from the literature.

Results and discussion The data needed for the assessment of soil erosion and soil carbon decline was in the main readily available at farm level in the Finnish agricultural database, the Finnish Meteorological Institute, and from geographic infor- mation systems. However, more data would have been needed in the cultivation history of the field parcels for an accurate assessment of soil carbon decline. With regard to soil compac- tion, we had difficulties in finding suitable data especially on machinery use, which is not available in public data sources.
Moreover, the reliability of the compaction model COMPSOIL was questioned, as its performance has not been validated in Finnish conditions.

Conclusions The erosion and SOM models were found to be suitable for use in LCAs. However, the model for the assessment of compaction was considered too difficult to use. Furthermore, the compaction model results need to be validated with field measurements to be considered reliable in Finnish conditions. This study provides a starting point for developing soil quality assessment in Finnish agricultural production.
Responsible editor: Llorenc Milà i Canals
Keywords Cereal . Compaction . Erosion . Life cycle assessment . Soil organic carbon . Turnip rape

3: 1.1 Soil quality, indicators and threats in Europe and Finland

Soil quality has been identified as a primary indicator for sustainable land management due to its crucial role in sustain- ing biomass production, nutrient and carbon cycling and maintenance of air and water quality (Doran 2002). A widely used definition describes soil quality as a soil's capacity to function (Karlen et al. 1997). The eight main threats to soil functioning as identified in the Communication of the European Commission "Towards a thematic strategy on soil protection" are erosion, organic matter decline, contamina- tion, salinization, compaction, soil biodiversity loss, sealing, landslides and flooding (2002). In the proposal for a directive for the protection of soil (EC 2006), all of these except soil biodiversity loss and flooding are included. For soil biodiver- sity, it is stated that the current scientific knowledge is too
1 Natural Resources Institute Finland, Latokartanonkaari 9, 00790 Helsinki, Finland
limited to allow for a specific provision. The prevention of floods is addressed by another directive proposal. Erosion is also listed as a soil quality indicator by the OECD (2013).

In Finland, Yli-Viikari et al. (2002) have proposed four indicators to be used in national soil quality monitoring. These include nutrient status (P mg acidity (pH), organic matter content (org. C %) and content of the heavy metal cadmium (mg Cd Yli-Viikari et al. (2002) also stressed the need to develop indicators for soil structural deterioration (compaction) and soil biological quality (micro- and macroorganisms). In a review of on-farm quality evaluation methods, Kukkonen et al. (2004) concluded that the biggest risk factors for soil quality in Finland are the reduction of organic matter and compaction. The Finnish on-farm soil quality test (Myllys et al. 2011) includes soil chemical prop- erties and several indicators related to soil's biological and physical properties, e.g. soil respiration, number of earthworms and water permeability of topsoil and subsoil.

5: <header>1.2 Soil quality in life cycle assessment </header>

As Garrigues et al. (2012a) say "soils are an essential compo- nent of the global ecosystem and are as important as the hy- drosphere and atmosphere for its functioning". These three components have their own functions and internal processes, but they also interact with each other. Nevertheless, all these essential components of the global ecosystem should be ad- dressed in life cycle assessment (LCA).

However, there is currently no established globally appli- cable method for assessing soil quality in LCA, and even discussion on the relevance of the issues is going on (Garrigues et al. 2012a). Several suggestions to incorporating soil quality impact into LCA have been made from different perspectives, as shown in Table 1. In these suggestions, soil quality has been treated as a separate midpoint impact catego- ry (Cowell and Clift 2000; Garrigues et al. 2012a) or in the context of land use impact (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b).

The United Nations Environment Programme/Society of Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry (UNEP/SETAC) Life Cycle Initiative (Milà i Canals et al. 2007a) sought a consensus method by proposing a wide conceptual framework for an assessment of the land use impact, which addresses transformation and occupation impacts, i.e. the impacts during the life span of certain land use type for human use. Transformation impact includes net impact of an introduction of a land use type and a (partial) recovery of soil functions after abandonment of the land use type. Occupation impact includes impacts on soil functions during the use of land to the considered purpose. Soil organic matter (SOM) is proposed as an impact indicator (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b). Method of Milà i Canals et al. (2007b) is also included, for example, in the International Reference Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) handbook Recommendations for life cycle impact assessment

in the European context as a recommended method for assess- ment of land use impact RC 2011). Despite this contribution, there is a lack of application of appropriate LCIA methods for assessing land use impacts on LCA (Garrigues et al. 2012a).

In this study, we apply methods to assess selected impacts during the occupation phase of agricultural land use and to test the applicability of these methods. Consideration of occupa- tion impacts allows to predict future transformation impacts and to correct land use measures during land use (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b). It is particularly important to maintain agricultural soil quality as agricultural soils are becoming more and more scarce resource per capita globally. In this respect, agricultural soil quality impact relates even more to a socioeconomic than ecologic system, although soil is an essential component of global ecosystem and soil quality is linked to other (ecological) environmental impacts, such as global warming potential and water eutrophication.

Erosion, soil organic matter and compaction were selected as impact indicators because they refer to processes that can improve or degrade the soil in the occupation phase of agri- cultural land use and because of their importance at the na- tional and European level (Yli-Viikari et al. 2002; EC 2002, 2006; Kukkonen et al. 2004). A similar framework was re- cently used by Garrigues et al. (2012b), who argued that pro- cesses are easier to relate to functional units than soil's phys- ical, chemical and biological properties or functions.

Erosion can be a considerable problem especially in south- ern Finland's clayey slope fields (Uusitalo et al. 2007). In addition to climatic conditions and field properties (soil tex- ture, slope), tillage and the presence or absence of vegetation cover in late autumn and winter have a great effect on the susceptibility of the soil to erosion (Puustinen et al. 2005). The majority of erosion occurs outside the growing season (Puustinen et al. 2007). Erosion risk is predicted to increase in the future due to milder winters and increased precipitation occurring outside the vegetation period, caused by climate change (Hildén et al. 2005).

According to Heikkinen et al. (2013), the content of organ- ic matter of Finnish cultivated soils has continuously de- creased by C. 220 kg ha year over the period 1974-2009. Between 1998 and 2009, carbon content in mineral soils de- clined in all management groups (annual, perennial, rotation), but especially in annual crops. In addition to the change in management practices in recent decades toward increasing cultivation of annual crops, other possible reasons for the ob- served C loss include climate change and the relatively late establishment of Finnish cultivated land. A large share of field area has been cleared as late as in the twentieth century.

Soil compaction is caused primarily by the use of heavy machinery in wet conditions (Alakukku et al. 2003). Excess moisture is most likely in clay soils that let water through slowly due to their fine texture. On clayey soils in Finland, winter frost is known to improve soil structure in the upper

soil layers in clayey fields if the soil is ploughed in the autumn. If the clayey soils are instead ploughed in the spring or direct seeded, frost does not have an effect. Clay soils are dominant especially in the southern parts of the country where according to the statistics of the Finnish soil fertility measurement, 58% of agricultural soils are clay, clay loam or silty clay loam (Eurofins Viljavuuspalvelu 2010). These soil texture classes are grouped together as clay in the Finnish soil texture classi- fication. In the whole country, the share of these soils is 28%.

The compaction of subsoil is regarded as an especially severe threat to agricultural production, since it is reported to considerably decrease yields of several crop species (Alakukku and Elonen 1994; Alakukku 2000) and to be long-lasting (Alakukku 2000). Water infiltration is impaired in fields with compacted subsoils, which increases surface runoff and topsoil erosion (Fullen 1985). Poor drainage causes problems for crop growth especially in wet years (Alakukku and Elonen 1994). Soil compaction can also contribute to greenhouse gas emissions by increasing N2O flows from de- nitrification (Ball 2013).

Attempts have been made to integrate the impacts of global land use on biodiversity and ecosystems into LCA as a part of the UNEP/SETAC Life Cycle Initiative (Koellner et al. 2013a). In keeping with these initiatives, the present study addresses the land occupation impacts caused by crop produc- tion on agricultural land. Our assessment was performed in great geographical detail using field parcel-specific data, which can be classified as level 5 regionalized geographical information, according to Koellner et al. (2013b).

As Garrigues et al. (2012a) say "soils are an essential compo- nent of the global ecosystem and are as important as the hy- drosphere and atmosphere for its functioning". These three components have their own functions and internal processes, but they also interact with each other. Nevertheless, all these essential components of the global ecosystem should be ad- dressed in life cycle assessment (LCA).

However, there is currently no established globally appli- cable method for assessing soil quality in LCA, and even discussion on the relevance of the issues is going on (Garrigues et al. 2012a). Several suggestions to incorporating soil quality impact into LCA have been made from different perspectives, as shown in Table 1. In these suggestions, soil quality has been treated as a separate midpoint impact catego- ry (Cowell and Clift 2000; Garrigues et al. 2012a) or in the context of land use impact (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b).

The United Nations Environment Programme/Society of Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry (UNEP/SETAC) Life Cycle Initiative (Milà i Canals et al. 2007a) sought a consensus method by proposing a wide conceptual framework for an assessment of the land use impact, which addresses transformation and occupation impacts, i.e. the impacts during the life span of certain land use type for human use. Transformation impact includes net impact of an introduction of a land use type and a (partial) recovery of soil functions after abandonment of the land use type. Occupation impact includes impacts on soil functions during the use of land to the considered purpose. Soil organic matter (SOM) is proposed as an impact indicator (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b). Method of Milà i Canals et al. (2007b) is also included, for example, in the International Reference Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) handbook Recommendations for life cycle impact assessment

in the European context as a recommended method for assess- ment of land use impact RC 2011). Despite this contribution, there is a lack of application of appropriate LCIA methods for assessing land use impacts on LCA (Garrigues et al. 2012a).

In this study, we apply methods to assess selected impacts during the occupation phase of agricultural land use and to test the applicability of these methods. Consideration of occupa- tion impacts allows to predict future transformation impacts and to correct land use measures during land use (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b). It is particularly important to maintain agricultural soil quality as agricultural soils are becoming more and more scarce resource per capita globally. In this respect, agricultural soil quality impact relates even more to a socioeconomic than ecologic system, although soil is an essential component of global ecosystem and soil quality is linked to other (ecological) environmental impacts, such as global warming potential and water eutrophication.

Erosion, soil organic matter and compaction were selected as impact indicators because they refer to processes that can improve or degrade the soil in the occupation phase of agri- cultural land use and because of their importance at the na- tional and European level (Yli-Viikari et al. 2002; EC 2002, 2006; Kukkonen et al. 2004). A similar framework was re- cently used by Garrigues et al. (2012b), who argued that pro- cesses are easier to relate to functional units than soil's phys- ical, chemical and biological properties or functions.

Erosion can be a considerable problem especially in south- ern Finland's clayey slope fields (Uusitalo et al. 2007). In addition to climatic conditions and field properties (soil tex- ture, slope), tillage and the presence or absence of vegetation cover in late autumn and winter have a great effect on the susceptibility of the soil to erosion (Puustinen et al. 2005). The majority of erosion occurs outside the growing season (Puustinen et al. 2007). Erosion risk is predicted to increase in the future due to milder winters and increased precipitation occurring outside the vegetation period, caused by climate change (Hildén et al. 2005).

According to Heikkinen et al. (2013), the content of organ- ic matter of Finnish cultivated soils has continuously de- creased by C. 220 kg ha year over the period 1974-2009. Between 1998 and 2009, carbon content in mineral soils de- clined in all management groups (annual, perennial, rotation), but especially in annual crops. In addition to the change in management practices in recent decades toward increasing cultivation of annual crops, other possible reasons for the ob- served C loss include climate change and the relatively late establishment of Finnish cultivated land. A large share of field area has been cleared as late as in the twentieth century.

Soil compaction is caused primarily by the use of heavy machinery in wet conditions (Alakukku et al. 2003). Excess moisture is most likely in clay soils that let water through slowly due to their fine texture. On clayey soils in Finland, winter frost is known to improve soil structure in the upper

soil layers in clayey fields if the soil is ploughed in the autumn. If the clayey soils are instead ploughed in the spring or direct seeded, frost does not have an effect. Clay soils are dominant especially in the southern parts of the country where according to the statistics of the Finnish soil fertility measurement, 58% of agricultural soils are clay, clay loam or silty clay loam (Eurofins Viljavuuspalvelu 2010). These soil texture classes are grouped together as clay in the Finnish soil texture classi- fication. In the whole country, the share of these soils is 28%.

The compaction of subsoil is regarded as an especially severe threat to agricultural production, since it is reported to considerably decrease yields of several crop species (Alakukku and Elonen 1994; Alakukku 2000) and to be long-lasting (Alakukku 2000). Water infiltration is impaired in fields with compacted subsoils, which increases surface runoff and topsoil erosion (Fullen 1985). Poor drainage causes problems for crop growth especially in wet years (Alakukku and Elonen 1994). Soil compaction can also contribute to greenhouse gas emissions by increasing N2O flows from de- nitrification (Ball 2013).

Attempts have been made to integrate the impacts of global land use on biodiversity and ecosystems into LCA as a part of the UNEP/SETAC Life Cycle Initiative (Koellner et al. 2013a). In keeping with these initiatives, the present study addresses the land occupation impacts caused by crop produc- tion on agricultural land. Our assessment was performed in great geographical detail using field parcel-specific data, which can be classified as level 5 regionalized geographical information, according to Koellner et al. (2013b).

12: In this study, the applicability of three soil quality indicators, erosion, organic matter and compaction, was assessed in the context of food LCA. To our view, the applicability of the method includes (1) possibility to allocate result to a function- al unit (FU), (2) user's practicability of assessment model, (3) availability of data, (4) fitting in the LCA's framework of environmental mechanism and existing indicators and (5) rel- evance of indicators from the point of view of the impacts from the entire life cycle. We tested the most potential avail- able assessment models to meet issues 1-3, while issues 4 and 5 are discussed in Section 3.4.

The test of the assessment models was done as a case study of four Finnish farms producing conventional and organic cereals and turnip rape, using farm-specific data from selected field parcels. The test assessment did not cover other phases of life cycles.
The functional unit used in the study was a metric ton of turnip rape or cereal seeds after harvesting. The results are also
presented per hectare and by year. In the present study, only on-farm field parcels were included, but the scope of the as- sessment could also be broadened to include other field areas.

Field parcels with four different arable cropping rotations were selected for each farm (Table 2). Data collected from the farms included field operations (including dates), yields and soil texture. Data from the farms were completed with data from geographic information systems or from the literature. Farm-specific yield data are presented in Table 3, and sources of other data required in soil quality assessment models are described in the following sections. Conventional farms were studied from 2007 to 2010 and organic farms from 2008 to 2011. The conventional farms were located in the Kanta- Häme region approximately 20 km away from each other and the organic farms in the Etelä-Savo region approximately 30 km away from each other and 200 km to the east-south-east from the conventional farms.

To our view, the applicability of the method includes (1) possibility to allocate result to a function- al unit (FU), (2) user's practicability of assessment model, (3) availability of data, (4) fitting in the LCA's framework of environmental mechanism and existing indicators and (5) rel- evance of indicators from the point of view of the impacts from the entire life cycle. presented per hectare and by year. In the present study, only on-farm field parcels were included, but the scope of the as- sessment could also be broadened to include other field areas.

Farm-specific yield data are presented in Table 3, and sources of other data required in soil quality assessment models are described in the following sections. The conventional farms were located in the Kanta- Häme region approximately 20 km away from each other and the organic farms in the Etelä-Savo region approximately 30 km away from each other and 200 km to the east-south-east from the conventional farms.
14: Water soil erosion was modelled with the empirically based VIHMA model (Puustinen et al. 2010). The model has been designed for the estimation of erosion and nutrient load on Finnish arable land at the watershed scale, and the results have been found to be consistent with long-term observations in catchment monitoring. VIHMA is a suitable model for LCA as it takes into account all crucial factors from the point of view of the cultivation technique, and it is relatively easy to use. VIHMA takes into account the effects of soil texture, tillage practices, wintertime crop cover, field slope, buffer zones and constructed wetlands. Required input data include field area by slope steepness, soil texture, tillage practices and soil phosphorus levels, which are inserted into VIHMA as hectares. When estimating erosion alone, the soil phosphorus levels are not needed. The parcel-specific source information of the erosion assessment is shown in Table 4. VIHMA gives erosion values for the different field classes as average nation- al figures and, therefore, does not take into account, for exam- ple, the size and shape of field parcels.
15: We used the Yasso07 model (Tuomi et al. 2009) to assess SOM in the fields. Yasso07 was originally created to estimate soil carbon stock changes in Finnish forest soils, but its

performance in agricultural conditions has been validated by Karhu et al. (2012). The model requires climatic and manage- ment data: annual precipitation (mm), annual mean tempera- ture (C) and the mean temperature of the warmest and the coldest month. The amount and chemical quality of carbon added to the soil in residue and organic amendments in the modelled years are also required, as well as the soil carbon stock at the beginning of the simulation. The chemical quality of carbon input is described by dividing it into four compart- ments based on compound groups: compounds soluble in a non-polar solvent, ethanol or dichloromethane (compartment E), or in water (W) and compounds hydrolysable in acid (A) and neither soluble nor hydrolysable at all (Ns). Each com- partment has its own decomposition rate. Decomposition re- sults in mass loss from the system (as carbon dioxide or dis- solved organic carbon), mass flows between the compart- ments as well as formation of more recalcitrant humus (H) (Tuomi et al. 2009). The model has been parameterized to the depth of 1 m, but the carbon stock can also be simulated in a thinner soil layer.
If the initial C stock is unknown, it can be estimated as a basis of the carbon input for 1 year, assuming the soil to be in steady state with the residue input.

Farm-specific weather and yield data used for the model- ling of the carbon stock in the initial state was obtained from the Finnish Meteorological Institute (2013a, mean values for the normal period 1981-2010 of each area), and farm-specific weather data used for the modelling of the change of the car- bon stock was obtained from the open data sets of the Finnish Meteorological Institute (2013b). Annual precipitations, aver- age temperatures and the mean temperature of the warmest and the coldest month at the four farms are presented in Table 5. The organic farms are located close to each other, SO data from the same weather station is used for both of them.

The carbon input from plant residue was calculated based on the yield level, dry matter content, harvest index and shoot- to-root ratio as described by Karhu et al. (2012). The harvest indexes and dry matter contents of the plants were obtained from Pahkala et al. (2009). We used the shoot-to-root values for spring wheat and green manure ley from Bolinder et al. (2007) and for barley, oats and turnip rape from Pietola and Alakukku (2005). For the pea-oats mixture, the mean of Jensen's (1987) value for pea and Pietola and Alakukku's (2005) value for oat was calculated. Since the actual herbage biomass of green manure leys was unknown, values from

Farm|Yield year-1|Yield year-1|Yield year-1|Yield year-1|Yield year-1 Farm|Turnip rape|Oats|Barley|Spring wheat|Pea-oats mixture Conventional farm 1|0.7|5.0|3.7|| Conventional farm 2|1.5|3.1|4.6|3.9| Organic farm 1|0.7|||4.0|3.5 Organic farm 2|0.9|||2.2|

Riesinger (2010) were used for the establishment year and values from Riesinger and Herzon (2008) for the subsequent years. Green manure leys were established with nurse crops. According to the data obtained from the farms, all plant resi- dues were assumed to be left on the field. The breaking down of ley roots was assumed to start on the year the ley was terminated. The ley years were assumed to take place before the cereal years. The farms did not report any adding of ma- nure on the studied field parcels, SO the effect of manure was omitted from this study.

Soil carbon content is routinely analysed on Finnish farms as a part of the soil fertility analysis that is performed every 5 years. The analysis is, however, not accurate enough to be used as an initial state of SOM, as it is done visually and as a rough esti- mate. We estimated the initial state of SOM by entering a car- bon input value that resulted in an initial C stock of similar size for all farms and assumed the soil to be in a steady state with the residue input. In this way, the effects of farming practices are more comparable within farms. The initial C stock was chosen as 53,000 kg ha-1, as it results in a C input from a realistic oats yield of 4750 kg ha for the studied conventional field parcels. Due to differences in weather conditions, an equivalent C stock was achieved on the organic field parcels with a lower yield level of 4600 kg ha-1. The initial C stock is equivalent to an organic matter content of 2% (calculated from a 20-cm-deep soil layer with formula: soil C stock = C concentration X bulk density X depth, where bulk density = 1.52-0.280 X In [C con- centration (%)] (Heikkinen et al. 2013).

In this way, the effects of farming practices are more comparable within farms. The initial C stock was chosen as 53,000 kg ha-1, as it results in a C input from a realistic oats yield of 4750 kg ha for the studied conventional field parcels. Due to differences in weather conditions, an equivalent C stock was achieved on the organic field parcels with a lower yield level of 4600 kg ha-1. The initial C stock is equivalent to an organic matter content of 2% (calculated from a 20-cm-deep soil layer with formula: soil C stock = C concentration X bulk density X depth, where bulk density = 1.52-0.280 X In [C con- centration (%)] (Heikkinen et al. 2013).
17: We used the Yasso07 model (Tuomi et al. 2009) to assess SOM in the fields. Yasso07 was originally created to estimate soil carbon stock changes in Finnish forest soils, but its

performance in agricultural conditions has been validated by Karhu et al. (2012). The model requires climatic and manage- ment data: annual precipitation (mm), annual mean tempera- ture (C) and the mean temperature of the warmest and the coldest month. The amount and chemical quality of carbon added to the soil in residue and organic amendments in the modelled years are also required, as well as the soil carbon stock at the beginning of the simulation. The chemical quality of carbon input is described by dividing it into four compart- ments based on compound groups: compounds soluble in a non-polar solvent, ethanol or dichloromethane (compartment E), or in water (W) and compounds hydrolysable in acid (A) and neither soluble nor hydrolysable at all (Ns). Each com- partment has its own decomposition rate. Decomposition re- sults in mass loss from the system (as carbon dioxide or dis- solved organic carbon), mass flows between the compart- ments as well as formation of more recalcitrant humus (H) (Tuomi et al. 2009). The model has been parameterized to the depth of 1 m, but the carbon stock can also be simulated in a thinner soil layer.
If the initial C stock is unknown, it can be estimated as a basis of the carbon input for 1 year, assuming the soil to be in steady state with the residue input.

Farm-specific weather and yield data used for the model- ling of the carbon stock in the initial state was obtained from the Finnish Meteorological Institute (2013a, mean values for the normal period 1981-2010 of each area), and farm-specific weather data used for the modelling of the change of the car- bon stock was obtained from the open data sets of the Finnish Meteorological Institute (2013b). Annual precipitations, aver- age temperatures and the mean temperature of the warmest and the coldest month at the four farms are presented in Table 5. The organic farms are located close to each other, SO data from the same weather station is used for both of them.

The carbon input from plant residue was calculated based on the yield level, dry matter content, harvest index and shoot- to-root ratio as described by Karhu et al. (2012). The harvest indexes and dry matter contents of the plants were obtained from Pahkala et al. (2009). We used the shoot-to-root values for spring wheat and green manure ley from Bolinder et al. (2007) and for barley, oats and turnip rape from Pietola and Alakukku (2005). For the pea-oats mixture, the mean of Jensen's (1987) value for pea and Pietola and Alakukku's (2005) value for oat was calculated. 21: Soil compaction was assessed by the COMPSOIL model (O'Sullivan et al. 1999). Running the model requires data on

soil texture, initial bulk density and water content during field traffic, as well as the weight, tire dimensions and inflation pressure of the machinery. The model has not been validated with Finnish field measurements. However, unlike many other compaction models, it is relatively simple to use and has been used recently by Garrigues et al. (2013) to predict soil com- paction in France, Pakistan and Brazil.

The COMPSOIL model includes constants for two default soil types, sandy loam and clay loam. Based on the Finnish, international (Yli-Halla et al. 2000) and European Commission's (Finke et al. 2001) soil texture triangles, it was assumed that these resemble sufficiently the soil textures of this study reported by the farms as well as in the Harmonized World Soil Database (FAO et al. 2012). Field operations reported by the farms included sowing, harvesting and tilling, as well as spraying of plant protection products on the conventional farms and mowing of the green manure leys on organic farms. Table 6 presents farm-specific input data on soil texture classes, densi- ties and water holding capacities, as well as the mean number of field operations per growth period and crop.

The water content of the soil at the time of each field op- eration was calculated based on soil water holding capacity, daily weather data (temperature, precipitation and solar radia- tion) and the plant cover coefficient (Kc) as described by Garrigues et al. (2013). Soil water holding capacity was cal- culated based on soil density from the values presented by Al Majou et al. (2008). Climate data necessary for the calcula- tions was obtained from the Finnish Meteorological Institute (2013b, c) (data from the nearest weather station). For the calculation of soil moisture, values for the plant cover coeffi- cient (Kc) by plant species and crop development stage and also by the lengths of the development stages were obtained from the FAO calculation guidelines for crop
Table 4 Farm averages of field slope steepness and tilling practices

Farm|Soil texture and VIHMA classification|Slope (%)|Tilling practice Conventional farm 1|Clay|1.5|25% of the area directly seeded; otherwise, spring tillage Conventional farm 2|Fine sand and coarser|4.1|Autumn ploughing for 1 year; otherwise, spring tillage Organic farm 1|Fine sand and coarser|1.1|Spring tillage Organic farm 2|Fine sand and coarser|1.9|Autumn ploughing for 1 year; otherwise, spring tillage

Farm|Year|Annual precipitation (mm)|Annual mean temperature (C)|Mean temperature of the warmest month|Mean temperature of the coldest month Conventional farm 1|2007|674|6|17|-11 Conventional farm 1|2008|818|6|16|-1 Conventional farm 1|2009|504|5|16|-7 Conventional farm 1|2010|598|4|22|-13 Conventional farm 2|2007|659|6|17|-12 Conventional farm 2|2008|681|6|17|-1 Conventional farm 2|2009|493|5|17|-7 Conventional farm 2|2010|550|4|22|-13 Organic farms 1 and 2|2008|737|5|15|-4 Organic farms 1 and 2|2009|533|4|16|-9 Organic farms 1 and 2|2010|497|2|22|-16 Organic farms 1 and 2|2011|654|5|19|-15

evapotranspiration (Allen et al. 1998). For crops other than green manure, the total length of the growing period was cal- culated as the sum of the length of the development stages and the proportion of this per stage of this. The true length of the development stages was calculated by dividing the total growth time reported by the farms with the proportional length of the development stage.

The machine data were not available from the farms, SO the machine types and most frequent size classes were taken from the latest national statistics, which covers the years 2000- 2002 (MTT, Vakola 2004). During this period, the typical power class of tractors has changed from 61-70 to 81- 100 kW. Due to this variation, compaction was modelled ac- cording to three different scenarios: 61-70, 81-100 and 121- 150 kW. Typical weights, tire dimensions and recommended inflation pressures of the machines and implements were col- lected from agricultural trade magazines for the years 2007 and 2008 (Ryhmäesittely 2007a, b, c, d, e, f, 2008) as well as from the technical manual for Nokian agricultural tires (2010). The total weight of the tractor was assumed to be distributed 40-60% between the front and rear axles (Riipinen 2010), while 20% of the weight of a pulled
implement was assumed to be placed on the rear axle (Berglund et al. 2002).

According to Garrigues et al. (2013), the soil's initial bulk density was assumed to be the same for all field operations, except for the spraying of plant protection substances, which were assumed to follow the same tracks. Each subsequent pass therefore started with the final bulk density after the previous pass. Ploughing was assumed to cause compaction only below 30 cm.

The COMPSOIL model gives the resulting change of soil density, which is converted into a loss of soil pore volume (m³ as described by Garrigues et al. (2013). Compaction of topsoil (0-0.3 m) and subsoil (0.3-0.5 m) was calculated separately.

Based on the Finnish, international (Yli-Halla et al. 2000) and European Commission's (Finke et al. 2001) soil texture triangles, it was assumed that these resemble sufficiently the soil textures of this study reported by the farms as well as in the Harmonized World Soil Database (FAO et al. 2012). Field operations reported by the farms included sowing, harvesting and tilling, as well as spraying of plant protection products on the conventional farms and mowing of the green manure leys on organic farms. Table 6 presents farm-specific input data on soil texture classes, densi- ties and water holding capacities, as well as the mean number of field operations per growth period and crop.

The water content of the soil at the time of each field op- eration was calculated based on soil water holding capacity, daily weather data (temperature, precipitation and solar radia- tion) and the plant cover coefficient (Kc) as described by Garrigues et al. (2013). Soil water holding capacity was cal- culated based on soil density from the values presented by Al Majou et al. (2008). Climate data necessary for the calcula- tions was obtained from the Finnish Meteorological Institute (2013b, c) (data from the nearest weather station). For the calculation of soil moisture, values for the plant cover coeffi- cient (Kc) by plant species and crop development stage and also by the lengths of the development stages were obtained from the FAO calculation guidelines for crop

Farm|Year|Annual precipitation (mm)|Annual mean temperature (C)|Mean temperature of the warmest month|Mean temperature of the coldest month Conventional farm 1|2007|674|6|17|-11 Conventional farm 1|2008|818|6|16|-1 Conventional farm 1|2009|504|5|16|-7 Conventional farm 1|2010|598|4|22|-13 Conventional farm 2|2007|659|6|17|-12 Conventional farm 2|2008|681|6|17|-1 Conventional farm 2|2009|493|5|17|-7 Conventional farm 2|2010|550|4|22|-13 Organic farms 1 and 2|2008|737|5|15|-4 Organic farms 1 and 2|2009|533|4|16|-9 Organic farms 1 and 2|2010|497|2|22|-16 Organic farms 1 and 2|2011|654|5|19|-15

evapotranspiration (Allen et al. 1998). For crops other than green manure, the total length of the growing period was cal- culated as the sum of the length of the development stages and the proportion of this per stage of this. The true length of the development stages was calculated by dividing the total growth time reported by the farms with the proportional length of the development stage.

The machine data were not available from the farms, SO the machine types and most frequent size classes were taken from the latest national statistics, which covers the years 2000- 2002 (MTT, Vakola 2004). During this period, the typical power class of tractors has changed from 61-70 to 81- 100 kW. Due to this variation, compaction was modelled ac- cording to three different scenarios: 61-70, 81-100 and 121- 150 kW. Typical weights, tire dimensions and recommended inflation pressures of the machines and implements were col- lected from agricultural trade magazines for the years 2007 and 2008 (Ryhmäesittely 2007a, b, c, d, e, f, 2008) as well as from the technical manual for Nokian agricultural tires (2010). The total weight of the tractor was assumed to be distributed 40-60% between the front and rear axles (Riipinen 2010), while 20% of the weight of a pulled
implement was assumed to be placed on the rear axle (Berglund et al. 2002).

According to Garrigues et al. (2013), the soil's initial bulk density was assumed to be the same for all field operations, except for the spraying of plant protection substances, which were assumed to follow the same tracks. Each subsequent pass therefore started with the final bulk density after the previous pass. Ploughing was assumed to cause compaction only below 30 cm.

The COMPSOIL model gives the resulting change of soil density, which is converted into a loss of soil pore volume (m³ as described by Garrigues et al. (2013). Compaction of topsoil (0-0.3 m) and subsoil (0.3-0.5 m) was calculated separately.

Farm|Soil type/texture|Soil type/texture|Soil type/texture|"Bulk dm³) density (kg"|per year Number of field operations|per year Number of field operations|per year Number of field operations|per year Number of field operations Farm|Farm data|HWSD database|COMPSOIL defaults|"Bulk dm³) density (kg"|Turnip rape|Cereals|Pea-oats mixture|Green manure Conventional farm 1|Clay/clay loam|Fine|Clay loam|1.26|6|6|| Conventional farm 2|Sandy loam|Medium|Sandy loam|1.41|7|5|| Organic farm 1|Sandy loam|Medium|Sandy loam|1.32|4|6|2|1 Organic farm 2|Sandy loam|Medium|Sandy loam|1.32|4|5||2
Fig. 1 Effect of farming practices on erosion and organic matter loss per field area. Negative values stand for organic matter gain

3 and 4. The differences between erosion are related to tillage practices and field slope. Fields in conventional and organic farm 2 were ploughed in the autumn for 1 year, and field slope was about twice as steep in conventional farm 2 than in the other farms, which can be shown clearly in the results calculated per field area (Fig. 1). When expressed per yield mass, erosion is greatest in organic farm 2 because of the low yields (Fig. 2).

SOM increased in the organic farms, whereas it de- creased in conventional farms. Soil C change was affected by plant above- and below-ground litter accumulation and yield level. The use of green manure on the organic farms seems to have a positive effect.

Differences in compaction were mainly related to soil texture and moisture (Figs. 3 and 4). Soil texture was finest, and therefore, the initial bulk density was lowest in conventional farm 1. Soil moisture was highest during

field operations in conventional farm 1 and higher in or- ganic farm 1 than in farm 2, which can be seen in the results calculated per field area (Fig. 3). However, the weight of the machine also has a clear impact. The compacting effect of field operations was greatest in sce- nario 3 (heaviest machinery) as shown in Figs. 3 and 4. On the other hand, the impact of the number of field operations seems to be of less importance. As there was no spraying of plant protection products in organic farms, the number of field operations was about 50% smaller. When calculating the results per yield mass, compaction is greatest in organic farm 2 because of the low yields (Fig. 4). The results were not validated by measurements, SO they cannot be considered to describe the true situation in those fields or in Finland in general. Also, no conclu- sions can be drawn about the differences between organic and conventional farming.
Fig. 2 Effect of farming practices on erosion and organic matter loss per metric ton yield. Negative values stand for organic matter gain
Fig. 3 Effect of farming practices on compaction per field area. Scenarios 1-3 stand for tractor size classes 1 (61-70 kW), 2 (81- 100 kW) and 3 (121-150 kW)

Farm|Soil type/texture|Soil type/texture|Soil type/texture|"Bulk dm³) density (kg"|per year Number of field operations|per year Number of field operations|per year Number of field operations|per year Number of field operations Farm|Farm data|HWSD database|COMPSOIL defaults|"Bulk dm³) density (kg"|Turnip rape|Cereals|Pea-oats mixture|Green manure Conventional farm 1|Clay/clay loam|Fine|Clay loam|1.26|6|6|| Conventional farm 2|Sandy loam|Medium|Sandy loam|1.41|7|5|| Organic farm 1|Sandy loam|Medium|Sandy loam|1.32|4|6|2|1 Organic farm 2|Sandy loam|Medium|Sandy loam|1.32|4|5||2
Fig. 1 Effect of farming practices on erosion and organic matter loss per field area. Negative values stand for organic matter gain

3 and 4. The differences between erosion are related to tillage practices and field slope. Fields in conventional and organic farm 2 were ploughed in the autumn for 1 year, and field slope was about twice as steep in conventional farm 2 than in the other farms, which can be shown clearly in the results calculated per field area (Fig. 1). When expressed per yield mass, erosion is greatest in organic farm 2 because of the low yields (Fig. 2).

SOM increased in the organic farms, whereas it de- creased in conventional farms. Soil C change was affected by plant above- and below-ground litter accumulation and yield level. The use of green manure on the organic farms seems to have a positive effect.

Differences in compaction were mainly related to soil texture and moisture (Figs. 3 and 4). Soil texture was finest, and therefore, the initial bulk density was lowest in conventional farm 1. Soil moisture was highest during

field operations in conventional farm 1 and higher in or- ganic farm 1 than in farm 2, which can be seen in the results calculated per field area (Fig. 3). However, the weight of the machine also has a clear impact. The compacting effect of field operations was greatest in sce- nario 3 (heaviest machinery) as shown in Figs. 3 and 4. On the other hand, the impact of the number of field operations seems to be of less importance. As there was no spraying of plant protection products in organic farms, the number of field operations was about 50% smaller. When calculating the results per yield mass, compaction is greatest in organic farm 2 because of the low yields (Fig. 4). The results were not validated by measurements, SO they cannot be considered to describe the true situation in those fields or in Finland in general. Also, no conclu- sions can be drawn about the differences between organic and conventional farming.

32: The effect of farming practices on the erosion of field soils has been studied in several LCA studies (Table 7). In the majority, the effects of certain crops have been studied and the results are presented either per area or per product unit. As in the present study, Garrigues et al. (2012b), Mattsson et al. (2000) and Oberholzer et al. (2012) studied only the direct effect of farming on soil erosion. In this kind of approach, soil quality is defined as the soils ability to maintain a good yield level in the long term.

Values for the erosion effect of the cultivation of turnip rape presented by Mattsson et al. (2000) and for the production of a composite feed product presented by Garrigues et al. (2012b) are on the same level as the results obtained in this study for conventional and organic farm 1. The results of Mattsson et al.

(2000) are based on field measurements, whereas Garrigues et al. (2012b) used the RUSLE 2 model. The use of RUSLE is possible also in Finland, and it would allow more specific analysis based on field characteristics (Renard and Ferreira 1993). However, RUSLE has been shown to be currently suit- able for assessments of larger areas, e.g. catchments (Lilja et al. 2014), but on field parcel scale, the effects of different cultivation practices cannot yet be assessed reliably. Work is under way to adjust the so-called C and R factors describing rainfall-runoff erosivity and soil cover management into Finnish conditions. In the future, RUSLE may, however, be the preferable model for erosion assessments in Finland.

The method used by Mila i Canals et al. (2013) for the assessment of soil erosion regulation potential differs greatly from the method used by Garrigues et al. (2012b) and in this study. It considers the effect of erosion on ecosystem services on a broad scale, not only in regard to crop production. In the calculation, the direct effect of farming, the erosion level in a
Fig. 4 Effect of farming practices on compaction per metric ton yield. Scenarios 1-3 stand for tractor size classes 1 (61-70 kW), 2 (81-100 kW) and 3 (121- 150 kW)

natural reference vegetation type (in Finnish conditions, a co- niferous forest) and the time period during which an aban- doned field is assumed to be restored to a vegetation-type equivalent to the natural state are all taken into account. This calculation method gives much greater erosion values than have been obtained in other studies. This method initially presented by Saad et al. (2013) has been accepted by the UNEP/SETAC as describing the potential regulation of soil erosion (Koellner et al. 2013a).

In contrast to other authors, Oberholzer et al. (2012) stud- ied whole farming systems by classifying the aggregated ef- fect of individual farming practices into a non-numerical scale (--, 0, +, ++).

The VIHMA model has been designed for watershed-scale assessments (Puustinen et al. 2010). Although it is used at the field parcel level in this study, it does not take into account annual weather conditions or the size and shape of field par- cels, SO it does not describe an actual situation in the field. It can, however, be considered suitable for LCAs since it takes into account all factors that are crucial from the point of view of cultivation technique. VIHMA was easy to use, as data on soil texture and tilling practices were readily available from the farms. Field slope was also easily available with the use of geographic information systems. Our results are relatively close to the averages of long-term measurements on experi- mental fields with comparable field and tillage characteristics, as shown in Table 8.

(2012b) are on the same level as the results obtained in this study for conventional and organic farm 1. The results of Mattsson et al.

(2000) are based on field measurements, whereas Garrigues et al. (2012b) used the RUSLE 2 model. The use of RUSLE is possible also in Finland, and it would allow more specific analysis based on field characteristics (Renard and Ferreira 1993). However, RUSLE has been shown to be currently suit- able for assessments of larger areas, e.g. catchments (Lilja et al. 2014), but on field parcel scale, the effects of different cultivation practices cannot yet be assessed reliably. Work is under way to adjust the so-called C and R factors describing rainfall-runoff erosivity and soil cover management into Finnish conditions. In the future, RUSLE may, however, be the preferable model for erosion assessments in Finland.

The method used by Mila i Canals et al. (2013) for the assessment of soil erosion regulation potential differs greatly from the method used by Garrigues et al. (2012b) and in this study. It considers the effect of erosion on ecosystem services on a broad scale, not only in regard to crop production. In the calculation, the direct effect of farming, the erosion level in a

natural reference vegetation type (in Finnish conditions, a co- niferous forest) and the time period during which an aban- doned field is assumed to be restored to a vegetation-type equivalent to the natural state are all taken into account. This calculation method gives much greater erosion values than have been obtained in other studies. This method initially presented by Saad et al. (2013) has been accepted by the UNEP/SETAC as describing the potential regulation of soil erosion (Koellner et al. 2013a).

In contrast to other authors, Oberholzer et al. (2012) stud- ied whole farming systems by classifying the aggregated ef- fect of individual farming practices into a non-numerical scale (--, 0, +, ++).

The VIHMA model has been designed for watershed-scale assessments (Puustinen et al. 2010). Although it is used at the field parcel level in this study, it does not take into account annual weather conditions or the size and shape of field par- cels, SO it does not describe an actual situation in the field. It can, however, be considered suitable for LCAs since it takes into account all factors that are crucial from the point of view of cultivation technique. VIHMA was easy to use, as data on soil texture and tilling practices were readily available from the farms. Field slope was also easily available with the use of geographic information systems. Our results are relatively close to the averages of long-term measurements on experi- mental fields with comparable field and tillage characteristics, as shown in Table 8.
38: In previous soil quality and land use LCAs, SOM has been the most frequently used as indicator (Table 1). Similar to assess- ments of erosion, some authors have studied only the direct

effect of farming on SOM (Mattsson et al. 2000; Garrigues et al. 2012b; Oberholzer et al. 2012), as was done in the pres- ent study. Garrigues et al. (2012b) assessed the organic matter change with a largely similar approach to ours, using the RothC model (Coleman et al. 1997). On the other hand, Mattsson et al. (2000) only gave a qualitative estimate for SOM change caused by farming, while Oberholzer et al. (2012) studied whole farming systems by classifying the ag- gregated effect of individual farming practices on a non- numerical scale (- -, 0, +, ++). The value presented by Garrigues et al. (2012b) for soil carbon stock changes caused by the production of composite feed products is within the range of the results obtained in this study.

In contrast to Garrigues et al.'s study, Brandão et al. (2011), Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b, 2013) and Mattila et al. (2011, 2012) used a method in which the difference between the organic matter level in the arable field and in a natural refer- ence vegetation type was taken into account in addition to the direct effect of farming. The method was originally presented by Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b).

Brandão et al. (2011), Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b, 2013) and Mattila et al. (2011, 2012) assessed the loss of SOM caused by farming practices to be much greater than in the current study. This is due to the different calculation method, which considers the effect of organic matter on a broad scale on ecosystem services, similar to the method of Saad et al. (2013) for erosion assessment. Brandão et al. (2011) also gives an estimate for the annual carbon stock change during farming (-0.24 which is based on a literature refer- ence. This value is within the range of our results.
Farming has previously been found to have a mainly neg- ative impact on soil C stocks (Brandão et al. 2011; Garrigues et al. 2012b; Milà i Canals et al. 2013; Mattila et al. 2011,
Table 8 Comparison of field parcel-specific results with averages of long-term measurements on experimental fields (Puustinen et al. 2010)

Farm/experimental field|Soil texture|Slope (%)|Tilling practice|Erosion Conventional farm 1|Clay|1,8|Stubble|605 Kotkanoja|Clay|2|Stubble|600 Organic farm 1|Fine sand and coarser|0,5|Spring tillage|205 Organic farm 2|Fine sand and coarser|1,5|Spring tillage|352 Toholampi|Coarse silt|0,5|Ley|340 Organic farm 2|Fine sand and coarser|1,5|Autumn ploughing|1065 Toholampi|Coarse silt|0,5|Autumn ploughing|690
2012), but positive results have also been reported (Milà i Canals et al. 2007a, b; Oberholzer et al. 2012).

The results from this study are on a reasonable level com- pared to those from previous studies, even though they cannot be considered fully reliable. We had difficulties especially in defining the soil carbon stocks in the initial state. The assump- tion that the soil would have been in a steady state with the residue input hardly holds true. A considerable proportion of Finnish agricultural land has been cleared as late as the twen- tieth century, and reaching a steady state can take a consider- able period of time (Heikkinen et al. 2013). The data on field management practices used in this study only covered a short period of time. On the other hand, the relative differences between the modelled field parcels hold true and thus the assessment reflects a potential impact.

The exact reason for the long-term declining trend in or- ganic matter in Finnish agricultural soils is unknown (Heikkinen et al. 2013) and cannot, thus, be properly taken into account in modelling. In cultivated mineral soils, the C stock has decreased at the rate of 220 kg C ha year -1 during the period 1974-2009. In order to obtain reliable results with the Yasso07 model, the timing of the land clearance and the quantity and quality of annual C input from the forest vegeta- tion preceding clearance should be known. In that case, the initial soil carbon stock could be assessed based on the residue input of the typical forest type in the region. Thereafter, the effect of land use change could also be taken into account. An alternative approach would be to add data on soil C pools SO that a current stock as well as its distribution in the pools.

According to Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b), to take into account the medium- to longer-term effects of the studied agricultural management practices on soil C stocks, the longest possible time period should be used. The results should then be allocated amongst the overall yield of all the years. In their study, the time period is 23 years. Also, Garrigues et al. (2012a, b) have used the same principle, and the time period in their study is 20 years. To analyse the effect of the time period, we also assessed soil C loss using a 20-year time period, with the assumptions that the crop rotations, yields and weather conditions would be repeated four times. Both the SOM losses on the conventional farms and the SOM gains on

the organic farms were smaller when a longer time period was used in the assessment (Fig. 5). However, the effect of the duration of the green manure ley becomes clearer in the longer time perspective.

Weather data were available from the Finnish Meteorological Institute and crop residue input could be calcu- lated based on yield level, dry matter content, harvest index and shoot-to-root ratio. The residue input of green manure could not be estimated based on farm data, SO literature values had to be used. To include all carbon that ends up in the soil through plants, the root exudates should also be taken into account (Bolinder et al. 2007). In this study, these were excluded, be- cause of the difficulty in determining their chemical quality.

In that case, the initial soil carbon stock could be assessed based on the residue input of the typical forest type in the region. Thereafter, the effect of land use change could also be taken into account. An alternative approach would be to add data on soil C pools SO that a current stock as well as its distribution in the pools.

According to Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b), to take into account the medium- to longer-term effects of the studied agricultural management practices on soil C stocks, the longest possible time period should be used. The results should then be allocated amongst the overall yield of all the years. In their study, the time period is 23 years. Also, Garrigues et al. (2012a, b) have used the same principle, and the time period in their study is 20 years. To analyse the effect of the time period, we also assessed soil C loss using a 20-year time period, with the assumptions that the crop rotations, yields and weather conditions would be repeated four times. Both the SOM losses on the conventional farms and the SOM gains on

the organic farms were smaller when a longer time period was used in the assessment (Fig. 5). However, the effect of the duration of the green manure ley becomes clearer in the longer time perspective.

Weather data were available from the Finnish Meteorological Institute and crop residue input could be calcu- lated based on yield level, dry matter content, harvest index and shoot-to-root ratio. The residue input of green manure could not be estimated based on farm data, SO literature values had to be used. To include all carbon that ends up in the soil through plants, the root exudates should also be taken into account (Bolinder et al. 2007). In this study, these were excluded, be- cause of the difficulty in determining their chemical quality.
43: In previous soil quality and land use LCAs, SOM has been the most frequently used as indicator (Table 1). Similar to assess- ments of erosion, some authors have studied only the direct

effect of farming on SOM (Mattsson et al. 2000; Garrigues et al. 2012b; Oberholzer et al. 2012), as was done in the pres- ent study. Garrigues et al. (2012b) assessed the organic matter change with a largely similar approach to ours, using the RothC model (Coleman et al. 1997). On the other hand, Mattsson et al. (2000) only gave a qualitative estimate for SOM change caused by farming, while Oberholzer et al. (2012) studied whole farming systems by classifying the ag- gregated effect of individual farming practices on a non- numerical scale (- -, 0, +, ++). The value presented by Garrigues et al. (2012b) for soil carbon stock changes caused by the production of composite feed products is within the range of the results obtained in this study.

In contrast to Garrigues et al.'s study, Brandão et al. (2011), Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b, 2013) and Mattila et al. (2011, 2012) used a method in which the difference between the organic matter level in the arable field and in a natural refer- ence vegetation type was taken into account in addition to the direct effect of farming. The method was originally presented by Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b).

Brandão et al. (2011), Mila i Canals et al. (2007a, b, 2013) and Mattila et al. (2011, 2012) assessed the loss of SOM caused by farming practices to be much greater than in the current study. This is due to the different calculation method, which considers the effect of organic matter on a broad scale on ecosystem services, similar to the method of Saad et al. (2013) for erosion assessment. Brandão et al. (2011) also gives an estimate for the annual carbon stock change during farming (-0.24 which is based on a literature refer- ence. This value is within the range of our results.
Farming has previously been found to have a mainly neg- ative impact on soil C stocks (Brandão et al. 2011; Garrigues et al. 2012b; Milà i Canals et al. 2013; Mattila et al. 2011,

The results from this study are on a reasonable level com- pared to those from previous studies, even though they cannot be considered fully reliable. We had difficulties especially in defining the soil carbon stocks in the initial state. The assump- tion that the soil would have been in a steady state with the residue input hardly holds true. A considerable proportion of Finnish agricultural land has been cleared as late as the twen- tieth century, and reaching a steady state can take a consider- able period of time (Heikkinen et al. 2013). The data on field management practices used in this study only covered a short period of time. On the other hand, the relative differences between the modelled field parcels hold true and thus the assessment reflects a potential impact.

The exact reason for the long-term declining trend in or- ganic matter in Finnish agricultural soils is unknown (Heikkinen et al. 2013) and cannot, thus, be properly taken into account in modelling. Both the SOM losses on the conventional farms and the SOM gains on

45: The COMPSOIL model itself may not give reliable results for Finnish soils, whose behaviour possibly differs from British soils, for which it has been developed O'Sullivan et al. 1999). Nevertheless, this model was used because unlike many other compaction models, it is relatively simple to use and has been recently used by Garrigues et al. (2013) to predict soil com- paction in the LCA context. However, our results cannot be considered reliable but are mostly suggestive.
Our approach to assessment was to use site-specific and relevant data as much as possible. In modelling the
Fig. 5 Effect of the time period used in the assessment of soil C change on the results of organic matter loss per metric ton yield. Negative values stand for organic matter gain

compaction caused by field operations, there was a lack of farm-specific data. The machinery data had to be collected from the statistics and manufacturer's literature. Soil density was estimated based on database values. Soil moisture was estimated using a French calculation method and default values (Garrigues et al. 2013), whose reliability in Finnish conditions is unknown. The current method was found to be laborious, since the input data on soil moisture has to be first calculated elsewhere using daily weather data (temperature, precipitation, solar ra- diation) and plant evapotranspiration separately for every sin- gle field operation, in practice for the whole growing season. Assessing individual operations or growing seasons is still relatively easy but becomes considerably more time- consuming when a longer time period, as in this study, is considered. It would, however, be reasonable to analyze lon- ger time periods, as annual weather and yield variations can be great, at least in Finland.

Numeric values for the effects of farming practices on soil compacting have been previously presented only by Garrigues et al. (2012b,2013), with the same model as used in this study. Their result is in the range of the results obtained in this study for conventional farm 2. The lack of other LCA studies re- flects the difficulty in modelling soil compaction.

For soil compaction to become more frequent in LCAs, a model that would be easier to use and give reliable results would have to be available. It would preferably contain dis- tinct modules at least for the estimation of soil moisture cal- culation. Thus, the input data would be easier to obtain. The collaboration between several western European countries has recently given rise to a tool called Terranimo (www. soilcompaction.eu), which remains under development, for the on-farm assessment of compaction risk caused by

individual field operations. This tool requires input data on machine characteristics, crop species, date of the field operation and geographical location. This kind of a tool might also be developed for use in LCA studies in the future.

48: The soil quality LCA approach used in this study and origi- nally presented by Garrigues et al. (2012b) includes chemical (organic matter) and physical (erosion, compaction) aspects of soil quality that affect crop production in the long term. Biological aspects are not included due to the lack of knowl- edge of the effects of farming practices (Yli-Viikari et al. 2002; Brandão and Milà i Canals 2013). Oberholzer et al. (2012) have developed a calculation method for soil micro- and macroorganisms, but it cannot directly be applied outside Switzerland and the results obtained are not numerical. An additional assessment method is clearly needed, as soil organ- isms are mentioned in both the preparation work of the EU thematic strategy for soil protection (EC 2002) and the list of national soil quality indicators suggested by Yli-Viikari et al. (2002). One option is to complement the current methodology of the assessment of ecotoxic effects of plant protection prod- ucts (Rosenbaum et al. 2008; Räsänen et al. 2013) by adding an indicator for soil organisms. However, also the toxic effects of heavy metals and other contaminants that can end up in the soil from soil amendments, such as compost, manure and sewage sludge, or machinery, should be taken into account.

In the present study, the three soil indicators (erosion, or- ganic matter and compaction) are assessed separately, but it should be noted that they also impact each other. For example, organic matter has been shown to correlate with the formation of stabile soil aggregates, which help maintain good soil

51: <header>3.4 Applicability of the approach in the context of LCA </header>

We focused on three soil quality issues related to agricultural land use, i.e. erosion, SOM and compaction. Garrigues et al. (2012a) put these issues in the framework of LCA impact categories, forming a coherent hierarchy of the impact indica- tors (Fig. 6). In this hierarchy, each of the soil quality issues dealt in the study represents a separate midpoint impact indi- cator, which can jointly form the midpoint soil quality indica- tor together with some other soil quality issues, such as sali- nization, if relevant characterization will be established. Different issues are relevant in different conditions. The mid- point soil quality indicator, in turn, is seen as a subcategory for a midpoint land use impact indicator. Land use impact indica- tor relates to different endpoint indicators, such as resource scarcity and damage to ecosystem diversity.

Garrigues et al. (2012a) also described relevant data and type of flows for inventory related to the separate soil quality indicators. These are parallel to input data and output (result) of the assessment models used in the study to assess three selected soil quality issues.

The framework of Garrigues et al. (2012a) suggests a ver- satile structure of the indicator for soil quality impact. It is a more multifaceted manner compared to the use of solely SOM as a soil quality indicator related to land use impact proposed by Milà i Canals et al. (2007b) and recommended by the ILCD (JRC 2011). These proposals are based on partly different standing points. Indicator for assessing long-term land use related soil quality impacts including transformation and oc- cupation is practical to be based on change in SOM (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b), while assessing soil quality impacts dur- ing the occupation stage is feasible to be based a relevant range of indicators.

SOM is seen as a feasible indicator as it affects multiple soil properties, such as buffer capacity, soil structure and fertility 2011) and soil life activity (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b), and thus, it reflects also larger soil quality impacts. In this study, we, however, found that the results from erosion, SOM and compaction assessments describe independently impacts of agricultural practices on related soil quality aspects. Thus, they provide complementary information on soil im- pact, and they are relevant to be assessed separately.

The system boundary of our assessment was limited to on- farm practices as the scope of the study was on the develop- ment of an assessment method related to soil impacts of agri- cultural production. There are soil quality impacts caused also by other stages of the life cycle of food products. These

impacts are quite different from those dynamic soil quality aspects relevant to maintaining soil quality in the occupation phase of agricultural soil, and for example, the choice of the reference state is crucial in the assessment. Impacts and methods for these impacts should be considered separately in further research. Depending on the viewpoint, there might be several relevant impact indicators for land use and soil quality impacts in addition to, or instead of, SOM and other soil quality indicators dealt with in this study, such as net primary biomass production and indicator for biodiversity (Lindeijer 2000). At the moment, however, impacts from oth- er stages of life cycle of food products are supposed to be negligible compared to the impacts of agricultural part in the context of LCA in which impacts are allocated to the products.
52: <header>3.4 Applicability of the approach in the context of LCA </header>

We focused on three soil quality issues related to agricultural land use, i.e. erosion, SOM and compaction. Garrigues et al. (2012a) put these issues in the framework of LCA impact categories, forming a coherent hierarchy of the impact indica- tors (Fig. 6). In this hierarchy, each of the soil quality issues dealt in the study represents a separate midpoint impact indi- cator, which can jointly form the midpoint soil quality indica- tor together with some other soil quality issues, such as sali- nization, if relevant characterization will be established. Different issues are relevant in different conditions. The mid- point soil quality indicator, in turn, is seen as a subcategory for a midpoint land use impact indicator. Land use impact indica- tor relates to different endpoint indicators, such as resource scarcity and damage to ecosystem diversity.

Garrigues et al. (2012a) also described relevant data and type of flows for inventory related to the separate soil quality indicators. These are parallel to input data and output (result) of the assessment models used in the study to assess three selected soil quality issues.

The framework of Garrigues et al. (2012a) suggests a ver- satile structure of the indicator for soil quality impact. It is a more multifaceted manner compared to the use of solely SOM as a soil quality indicator related to land use impact proposed by Milà i Canals et al. (2007b) and recommended by the ILCD (JRC 2011). These proposals are based on partly different standing points. Indicator for assessing long-term land use related soil quality impacts including transformation and oc- cupation is practical to be based on change in SOM (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b), while assessing soil quality impacts dur- ing the occupation stage is feasible to be based a relevant range of indicators.

SOM is seen as a feasible indicator as it affects multiple soil properties, such as buffer capacity, soil structure and fertility 2011) and soil life activity (Milà i Canals et al. 2007b), and thus, it reflects also larger soil quality impacts. In this study, we, however, found that the results from erosion, SOM and compaction assessments describe independently impacts of agricultural practices on related soil quality aspects. Thus, they provide complementary information on soil im- pact, and they are relevant to be assessed separately.

The system boundary of our assessment was limited to on- farm practices as the scope of the study was on the develop- ment of an assessment method related to soil impacts of agri- cultural production. There are soil quality impacts caused also by other stages of the life cycle of food products. These

impacts are quite different from those dynamic soil quality aspects relevant to maintaining soil quality in the occupation phase of agricultural soil, and for example, the choice of the reference state is crucial in the assessment. Impacts and methods for these impacts should be considered separately in further research. Depending on the viewpoint, there might be several relevant impact indicators for land use and soil quality impacts in addition to, or instead of, SOM and other soil quality indicators dealt with in this study, such as net primary biomass production and indicator for biodiversity (Lindeijer 2000). At the moment, however, impacts from oth- er stages of life cycle of food products are supposed to be negligible compared to the impacts of agricultural part in the context of LCA in which impacts are allocated to the products.

55: Soil and its quality have a crucial role in supporting sustain- able agricultural production. In Finland, the importance of factors affecting soil fertility or chemical quality, such as nu- trient contents and acidity, has long been recognized. Recently, the physical (soil structure) and biological factors (e.g. microbial activity, earthworm number) have also started to gain attention.

Since the turn of the millennium, several LCA studies re- lated to soil quality issues have been published. SOM and erosion have SO far been the most frequently used indicators, but the importance of including soil structure-related indica- tors has been widely recognized (Cowell and Clift 2000; Garrigues et al. 2012a, 2013; Oberholzer et al. 2012). The methodology is not yet established.

In this article, we present a case study in which the applica- bility of three soil quality-describing indicators (erosion, organ- ic matter and compaction) and the available assessment methods are tested in the context of LCA. We used data from two conventional farms and two organic farms, which were supplemented with geographic information and literature data. Data was collected from 4-year-long crop rotations that includ- ed turnip rape and cereals. In previous international studies, numeric values for crop rotations that include several crop spe- cies have not been presented. Also, the effect of organic farming on soil quality has not previously been studied in the context of LCA. Our results are on the same level as those of a few pre- vious international studies. There is a large variation between the methods used, however, SO the results are not directly com- parable. In the context of this study, the results were not verified with field measurements, SO they cannot be considered as de- scribing the actual situation on the studied farms or in Finland in general. However, we obtained valuable information on the conditions and characteristics of the assessment methods. The models used for the assessment of erosion and organic matter assessment were found to be suitable for use in LCAs, although

the modelling of the change of organic matter needs some fur- ther refinement. In addition, in the future, the model RUSLE might be the preferable model for erosion assessments in Finland. The model used for compaction assessment, on the other hand, is cumbersome and cannot be considered reliable in Finnish conditions. This model should be validated with Finnish field measurements, or other methods that are more easy to use may become available in the future.

According to our results, each of the indicators used seems to respond to farming practices independently. This supports their use together: none can be left out without losing valuable information about the overall effects of farming.

Assessment of soil quality impacts during the occupation phase of agricultural land use is a very relevant sustainability issue related to food products as it gives valuable information to be utilized in soil management in maintaining soil productivity. However, also impacts in a larger context, the context of trans- formation and occupation of land, should be assessed across the stages of life cycle of food products. Both of these aspects need to be further researched and tested in applications.

Acknowledgements We thank Riikka Nousiainen for the calculation of the slope steepness of the field parcels for the erosion assessment and Jaakko Heikkinen for the guidance and comments on the soil C stock assessment. The following experts in soil science also offered their view- points in the selection of indicators and the assessment methods: Visa Nuutinen, Merja Myllys, Pentti Seuri, Laura Alakukku, Helinä Hartikainen and Markku Yli-Halla.
